title: Why we keep voice messages from people who died
summary: Reflect on why people keep recordings of someone's voice after that person has died, exploring what sound preserves that photographs or text cannot.
topic: Why we keep voice messages from people who died
prompt: Write 3 paragraphs (350–500 words) exploring why people keep voice recordings of someone who has died. Describe a specific voice message or recording, explain what makes the voice different from a photograph, and reflect on whether it is comforting or painful to listen. Use present simple for general truths, past simple for personal narrative, at least one concession with 'although' or 'while', and a modal verb to express possibility.
hintStarters: Most people keep photographs, but there is one type of recording that is harder to delete…
Although I have not listened to it in months, I know it is still there on my phone…
A voice does something that a photograph cannot — it arrives in real time, and for a moment…

hintVocab.meaningVi: giọng nói nhỏ dần rồi dừng lại
nhịp điệu riêng của lời nói
bám víu vào điều gì đó

sampleText:
When my uncle died two years ago, my cousin discovered an old voice note on her phone — a short message he had left asking whether she was free for dinner on a Tuesday. He said her name at the start the way he always did, slightly louder than necessary, as if he was not sure the phone was working. She has not deleted it. She probably never will.

A voice does something that a photograph cannot — it arrives in real time. When you look at a static image of someone, you see them frozen in a single moment. When you hear a voice note, you experience the particular rhythm of their speech, the way they trail off at the end of a sentence, the background noise of wherever they were standing. My uncle's message included the sound of a television in another room and a dog barking twice. These details were meaningless when he sent it. Now they make the recording irreplaceable, because they place him in a real afternoon that actually happened. Although photographs show us what a person looked like, a voice recording tells us how they moved through time.

Whether to listen is a different question. Some people play the recordings often and find them comforting, a way of holding onto something that might otherwise fade. Others find that listening compresses grief into a few seconds and makes it harder to bear. My cousin told me she had listened to her father's message once after he died, and then she put her phone face down and sat very still for a long time. She said she might listen again someday, but not yet. This seems right to me. A voice recording is not a cure for loss — it is more like a door that could be opened when you are ready. Keeping it means refusing to let the voice disappear before you are prepared to let it go.

bị đóng băng trong một khoảnh khắc duy nhất

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Present simple for general truths
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'A voice does something that a photograph cannot' dùng thì hiện tại đơn vì đây là nhận xét mang tính tổng quát và luôn đúng, không giới hạn ở một thời điểm cụ thể. Ở cấp B1, việc chuyển đổi giữa hiện tại đơn (sự thật chung) và quá khứ đơn (câu chuyện cá nhân) là kỹ năng quan trọng.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'A voice does something that a photograph cannot' uses the present simple because it is a general observation that is always true, not limited to a specific time. At B1, shifting between present simple (general truth) and past simple (personal narrative) is a key skill.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Modal 'might' for tentative future possibility
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'She said she might listen again someday, but not yet' dùng 'might' trong lời tường thuật gián tiếp để bày tỏ khả năng trong tương lai với sự không chắc chắn. 'Might' yếu hơn 'may' và phù hợp khi người nói không chắc về quyết định của mình.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'She said she might listen again someday, but not yet' uses 'might' in reported speech to express future possibility with uncertainty. 'Might' is weaker than 'may' and is appropriate when the speaker is unsure about their own decision.

sampleTranslationVi:
Khi chú tôi mất hai năm trước, người anh họ của tôi phát hiện một tin nhắn thoại cũ trên điện thoại của cô ấy — một tin nhắn ngắn ông ấy để lại hỏi cô ấy có rảnh tối thứ Ba không. Ông ấy gọi tên cô ấy lúc đầu theo cách ông ấy thường làm, to hơn một chút so với cần thiết, như thể ông không chắc điện thoại có hoạt động không. Cô ấy chưa xóa nó. Chắc cô ấy sẽ không bao giờ xóa.

Giọng nói làm điều gì đó mà một bức ảnh không thể — nó đến trong thời gian thực. Khi bạn nhìn vào một hình ảnh tĩnh của ai đó, bạn thấy họ bị đóng băng trong một khoảnh khắc duy nhất. Khi bạn nghe một tin nhắn thoại, bạn trải nghiệm nhịp điệu riêng của lời nói họ, cách giọng họ nhỏ dần ở cuối câu, tiếng ồn nền ở bất cứ nơi nào họ đang đứng. Tin nhắn của chú tôi bao gồm tiếng tivi trong phòng khác và tiếng chó sủa hai lần. Những chi tiết này vô nghĩa khi ông gửi đi. Bây giờ chúng khiến bản ghi âm không thể thay thế được, vì chúng đặt ông vào một buổi chiều thực sự đã xảy ra. Mặc dù những bức ảnh cho chúng ta thấy một người trông như thế nào, một bản ghi âm giọng nói cho chúng ta biết họ di chuyển qua thời gian như thế nào.

Có nên nghe lại hay không là một câu hỏi khác. Một số người thường xuyên phát lại các bản ghi âm và thấy chúng an ủi, một cách bám víu vào điều gì đó có thể sẽ phai mờ. Những người khác thấy việc nghe dồn nén nỗi đau vào vài giây và khiến nó khó chịu đựng hơn. Người anh họ của tôi kể rằng cô ấy đã nghe tin nhắn của bố một lần sau khi ông mất, rồi đặt điện thoại úp xuống và ngồi im lặng rất lâu. Cô ấy nói có thể cô sẽ nghe lại vào một ngày nào đó, nhưng chưa phải bây giờ. Điều này có vẻ đúng với tôi. Một bản ghi âm giọng nói không phải là thuốc chữa mất mát — nó giống như một cánh cửa có thể được mở khi bạn sẵn sàng hơn. Giữ nó có nghĩa là từ chối để giọng nói biến mất trước khi bạn sẵn sàng buông bỏ.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses present simple to state a general truth?
options: A voice does something that a photograph cannot.
A voice is doing something that a photograph cannot.
A voice did something that a photograph cannot.
A voice has done something that a photograph cannot.
mcQuestions.explanation: Present simple is used for general truths and observations that are always valid, not limited to a specific moment.
mcQuestions.hint: Use present simple for facts and general truths.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses 'might' correctly to express tentative future possibility?
options: She said she might listen again someday, but not yet.
She said she might to listen again someday, but not yet.
She said she might listening again someday, but not yet.
She said she mights listen again someday, but not yet.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Might' is always followed by the bare infinitive. 'Might' never takes '-s', 'to', or '-ing'.
mcQuestions.hint: Modal verbs are always followed by the bare infinitive.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'although' to introduce a concession?
options: Although photographs show us what a person looked like, a voice recording tells us how they moved through time.
Although photographs show us what a person looked like, but a voice recording tells us how they moved through time.
Although, photographs show us what a person looked like, a voice recording tells us how they moved through time.
Photographs show us what a person looked like, although a voice recording tells us how they moved through time too.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Although + clause, main clause' — no comma after 'although', no 'but' before the main clause.
mcQuestions.hint: No comma after 'although'; no 'but' in the main clause.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses present perfect 'has not deleted' to describe an action that has not happened up to now?
options: She has not deleted the voice note.
She did not deleted the voice note.
She has not delete the voice note.
She not has deleted the voice note.
mcQuestions.explanation: Present perfect negative: 'has not + past participle'. 'Deleted' is the past participle of 'delete'.
mcQuestions.hint: Present perfect negative = 'has/have not + past participle'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which phrase correctly uses 'as if' to describe a comparison?
options: He said her name as if he was not sure the phone was working.
He said her name as if he is not sure the phone was working.
He said her name as if not sure the phone was working.
He said her name as he was not sure if the phone was working.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'As if + past tense clause' describes a hypothetical or uncertain comparison in a past narrative context.
mcQuestions.hint: 'As if' is followed by a clause; in past narratives, use past tense in the 'as if' clause.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option correctly uses 'make + object + adjective' to express a result?
options: These details make the recording irreplaceable.
These details make the recording to be irreplaceable.
These details make the recording is irreplaceable.
These details making the recording irreplaceable.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Make + object + adjective' (without 'to be' or 'is'): 'make the recording irreplaceable'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Make + object + adjective' — no 'to be' between the object and adjective.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'whether' to introduce an indirect question?
options: Whether to listen is a different question.
Whether listening is a different question.
If to listen is a different question.
Whether to listen or not a different question.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Whether to + infinitive' is used as a noun phrase (subject of the sentence). 'If' cannot introduce an infinitive phrase used as a subject.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Whether to + infinitive' can function as the subject of a sentence. 'If' cannot.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option correctly uses 'hold onto' + gerund?
options: It is a way of holding onto something that might otherwise fade.
It is a way to hold onto something that might otherwise fade.
It is a way of hold onto something that might otherwise fade.
It is a way for holding onto something that might otherwise fade.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'A way of + gerund (-ing)' is the correct structure when 'way' means 'method'. 'A way to + infinitive' is also acceptable, but 'a way of holding' is more natural here.
mcQuestions.hint: 'A way of' is followed by a gerund. 'A way to' is followed by an infinitive. Both are correct.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'rather than' to contrast two things?
options: Keeping it means refusing to let the voice disappear before you are prepared to let it go.
Keeping it means to refuse to let the voice disappear before you are prepared to let it go.
Keeping it means refuse to let the voice disappear before you are prepared to let it go.
Keeping it means refused to let the voice disappear before you are prepared to let it go.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Mean + gerund (-ing)' describes what something involves or implies. 'Mean + infinitive' describes intention. Here, 'means refusing' correctly describes what the action of keeping implies.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Mean + gerund' = describes what something involves. 'Mean + infinitive' = describes intention.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option correctly uses 'find + object + adjective/participle' to describe a reaction?
options: Others find that listening compresses grief into a few seconds and makes it harder to bear.
Others find that listening compresses grief into a few seconds and make it harder to bear.
Others find that listening compress grief into a few seconds and makes it harder to bear.
Others finds that listening compresses grief into a few seconds and makes it harder to bear.
mcQuestions.explanation: Plural subject 'others' takes 'find'. In the 'that' clause, 'listening' (gerund) is the subject, taking singular verbs 'compresses' and 'makes'.
mcQuestions.hint: The gerund 'listening' acts as a singular subject inside the 'that' clause, so use 'compresses' and 'makes'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'place + object + preposition' to describe location in time?
options: They place him in a real afternoon that actually happened.
They place him on a real afternoon that actually happened.
They places him in a real afternoon that actually happened.
They are placing him into a real afternoon that actually happened.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Place someone in' a moment or period of time is the natural preposition. 'Place him on' does not work for abstract time periods.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Place someone in + time period' uses 'in', not 'on'.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Does keeping a voice recording help us grieve, or does it prevent us from accepting that someone is gone? Is there a difference between remembering someone and being unable to let them go?
